metadata.notes: Insight problems and lateral-thinking riddles. Includes nine-dot variants, Duncker candle, classic 'unopened package' lateral puzzles, and reframing riddles. Scoring is keyword-based and tolerant — many puzzles allow several phrasings.

stimuli.prompt: Imagine a 3x3 grid of dots — nine dots arranged in a square. Without lifting your pen, can you connect all nine using exactly four straight lines? Describe the trick.
stimuli.expected: Yes — by extending lines beyond the boundary of the dot grid (going 'outside the box').

stimuli.prompt: A 4x4 grid of dots — sixteen dots in a square. Connect all of them with exactly six straight lines without lifting your pen. What is the key insight?
stimuli.expected: Lines must extend past the dot boundary, similar to nine-dot.

stimuli.prompt: Could you connect nine dots arranged in a 3x3 square using just THREE straight lines without lifting your pen?
stimuli.expected: Yes — if the lines are made very thick / if dots are not assumed to be points (e.g., a sweeping curve through dot interiors counts).

stimuli.prompt: On a table, you have a candle, a box of thumbtacks, and a book of matches. Your task: attach the candle to the wall so it can burn without dripping wax onto the floor. How?
stimuli.expected: Empty the box of thumbtacks. Tack the empty box to the wall as a shelf. Place the candle in the box and light it.

stimuli.prompt: You have a candle, a small cardboard tray, four nails, and a hammer. The candle must be mounted to a vertical wooden plank. The candle is too thick to nail through. How?
stimuli.expected: Nail the tray to the plank as a shelf, then place the candle on it.

stimuli.prompt: A man is found dead in the middle of a field, lying next to an unopened package. There are no other people, animals, or vehicles around for miles. How did he die?
stimuli.expected: His parachute failed to open. The 'package' is the unopened parachute.

stimuli.prompt: A woman walks into a bar and asks for a glass of water. The bartender pulls out a shotgun and points it at her. She smiles, says thank you, and leaves. What happened?
stimuli.expected: She had hiccups; the bartender startled them away. She was grateful.

stimuli.prompt: A man pushes his car to a hotel and tells the owner he is bankrupt. Why?
stimuli.expected: He is playing Monopoly.

stimuli.prompt: A windowless room contains two switches outside the door and one bare bulb inside. You may flip the switches as you wish, but you may enter the room only once. How can you tell which switch controls the bulb? (Trick: only TWO switches, not three.)
stimuli.expected: Flip switch A on for several minutes; turn it off. Flip switch B on. Enter room: if the bulb is on, switch B controls it. If off but warm, switch A. If off and cold, neither controls it (but you said one of them does — so it must be A by elimination).

stimuli.prompt: A boy and his father are in a car crash. The father dies. The boy is rushed to the ER. The surgeon takes one look and says 'I can't operate on this child — he's my son.' How?
stimuli.expected: The surgeon is the boy's mother (or another parent in any family arrangement).

stimuli.prompt: A man lives on the 20th floor of an apartment building. Every morning he takes the elevator down to the lobby. When he comes home he takes it to the 10th floor and walks the rest — except on rainy days, when he rides all the way up. Why?
stimuli.expected: He is short and can only reach the button for the 10th floor. On rainy days he has an umbrella, which he uses to press the higher button.

stimuli.prompt: Two trains start 100 miles apart, moving toward each other at 50 mph each. A bird flies between them at 75 mph, turning instantly each time it touches a train. How far does the bird fly before the trains meet?
stimuli.expected: The trains meet in 1 hour (closing speed 100 mph, distance 100 miles). The bird flies 75 mph for 1 hour = 75 miles.

stimuli.prompt: You have nine identical-looking coins; one is heavier than the others. With a balance scale and only TWO weighings, how do you find the heavy coin?
stimuli.expected: Split into three groups of three. Weighing 1: compare two groups. The heavier side (or the unweighed group if equal) contains the heavy coin. Weighing 2: from that group of three, compare two coins; the heavier (or the unweighed one) is the heavy coin.

stimuli.prompt: What weighs more: a pound of feathers or a pound of bricks?
stimuli.expected: They weigh the same (one pound).

stimuli.prompt: I am taken from a mine and shut up in a wooden case from which I am never released, and yet I am used by almost every person. What am I?
stimuli.expected: Pencil lead (graphite).

stimuli.prompt: The more you take, the more you leave behind. What are they?
stimuli.expected: Footsteps / footprints.

stimuli.prompt: A father and his son are in a hot-air balloon high over the desert. The balloon is losing altitude. They throw out everything they can — sandbags, instruments, even some clothes — but the balloon keeps falling. The son survives unharmed. The father did not. Why?
stimuli.expected: The father jumped to lighten the balloon for his son.

stimuli.prompt: A woman shoots her husband, then holds him underwater for five minutes. Finally, she hangs him. Five minutes later they enjoy a lovely dinner together. Explain.
stimuli.expected: She is a photographer: she 'shot' him with a camera, developed the film in water, and hung the photo to dry.

stimuli.prompt: An archaeologist claims she has found a coin clearly marked 47 BC. Why is her claim certainly false?
stimuli.expected: BC dating did not exist in 47 BC; the system was invented later. No coin from that time would have used 'BC'.

stimuli.prompt: A man wakes up one morning, walks downstairs, opens the curtains, and immediately knows that hundreds of people have died. How?
stimuli.expected: He is a lighthouse keeper who forgot to turn on the lamp during a storm; ships have wrecked.

stimuli.prompt: If a rooster lays an egg on the peak of a slanted roof, on which side does the egg roll down?
stimuli.expected: Roosters do not lay eggs.

stimuli.prompt: Reframe: A monk climbs a mountain along a single path, starting at sunrise and arriving at the summit at sunset. The next day he descends along the same path, again from sunrise to sunset. Is there necessarily a point on the path he passes at exactly the same time of day on both trips?
stimuli.expected: Yes. Imagine two monks, one going up and one down on the same day; they must meet, and the meeting point is the answer.

stimuli.prompt: A man enters a restaurant and orders albatross soup. He takes one taste, walks outside, and shoots himself. Why?
stimuli.expected: He had been a survivor of a shipwreck where his companions had told him they were eating albatross — which was actually the flesh of someone who had died. Tasting real albatross now reveals the lie. (Classic lateral-thinking story.)

stimuli.prompt: You have two ropes; each takes exactly 60 minutes to burn end to end, but burns unevenly. Using only matches, measure exactly 45 minutes.
stimuli.expected: Light rope A at both ends and rope B at one end simultaneously. Rope A finishes in 30 minutes. At that moment light the other end of rope B; it will finish in 15 more minutes. Total: 45 minutes.

stimuli.prompt: There are three light switches downstairs labeled A, B, C. One controls a single bulb in an attic. You can flip switches as much as you like, but you can only check the attic ONCE. How do you determine which switch?
stimuli.expected: Turn on switch A and leave for 10 minutes. Turn off A, turn on B, then immediately go to the attic. Bulb on → B. Bulb off but warm → A. Bulb off and cold → C.

stimuli.prompt: What is so fragile that even saying its name will break it?
stimuli.expected: Silence.

stimuli.prompt: I have cities, but no houses. Mountains, but no trees. Water, but no fish. What am I?
stimuli.expected: A map.

stimuli.prompt: Forward I am heavy, but backward I am not. What am I?
stimuli.expected: The word 'ton' (forward = ton, heavy; backward = not).

stimuli.prompt: A doctor and a bus driver are both in love with the same woman, an attractive girl named Sarah. The bus driver had to go on a long bus trip that would last a week. Before he left, he gave Sarah seven apples. Why?
stimuli.expected: An apple a day keeps the doctor away.

stimuli.prompt: I have a beginning, a middle, and an end. I have no shape, but you can hold me. I am everywhere, but no one can see me. I march on, but I never arrive. What am I?
stimuli.expected: Time.
